Gentlemen:
I am writi-ng on behaLf of my cJ-ient, the Livermore Area
Recreation and Park District (hereinafter the "District"). 
I am
wrj.ting at the direction and with the authorization of the Board
of Directors to raise a question rlrhether one of the members of
our Board of Directors has a conffict of interest as defined by
the Act 
(Government Code Sections 81OOO-91015) and the
regulations adopted pursuant to that legistation.
Factual Background.
The Dj-strict is a special district 
formed pursuant to the
appropriate provisions of the public Resources Code. 
The
District j-s governed by a five member eLected Board of Directors.

its 
boundaries, and the
responsibility for their development and maintenance is delegated

The District owns several
park sites in its own narne includj,ng a 365 acre nature park
outside the City limits, but virtuaLly a1l, the neighborhood plrks
are situated on lands owned by the City and operated and
maintained by the District. 
The park sites are acquired by the
City through purchases, developer dedications and occasionalLy,
gifts. 
Under the JpA, any funds col-lected by the Cj.ty in the
form of developer in lieu fees are directly transferable to the
District for the construction of parks.
Mr. David Bing is a nember of the Board of Directors of the
Livermore Area Recreatj-on and Park District. 
Mr. Bing's address
is 71 Trevarno Road, Livermore, CA, 94550, the Districtrs office
and headquarters. Mr. Bing is a principal stockholder in white
Oak Landscape, a California corporation. White Oak hol-ds both a
general engineering contractorrs license (an "Ar, ticense) and a
specialty contractorrs license (a t,C-27r license, Iandscaping) .
For purposes of the contractor's ficensing Board, the responsible
managing officer in the corporation is Mr. Robert quatrnin. Mr.
Quatman is not otherwise invo1ved in the issue(s) being raised in
this letter.
White Oak has two divisions: Construction and Uaintenance.
White oak does not now and has never directly contracted for
construction or maintenance work with the District. 
One of the
types of projects \,/hich White Oak (as a subcontractor) can and
does submit bids for are 'turn keyrr parks built by deveiopers and
subseguently dedicated to the public entity having jurilaiction
for the developnent of the project and park in question.
Usually, title 
to the park is turned over to the public entity
through the dedication process, The parks are atways built 
by

rrdesign-buiId" basis.

jurisdiction_ for

Through custom and. habit and pursuant to the terns if the JpA; it

Board of Directors. 
In the case of residential projects, the
Board usually recommends whether the city 
shoul-d require
dedication of park land or in the alternative, the payrnent ot in
Lieu fees. 
The city of course is not bound by the District's
recomrnendat j.on but generally pays deference to its position.
Secondly, where the construction of a park is called for,
District staff reviews the developerrs plans and subrnittals for
conformance with the Districtrs 
Master pl-an and for 
the
appropriateness of the park Iocation and the equiprnent bej.ng
proposed. Fina1ly, as the park j,s being bui1t, District stafi
works with City staff to inspect (for conformance to the plans
and specifications) and approve the developerrs sub-contractorr s
work. 
In this regard, the City inspectors would inspect the
water and electrical portions of the )ob, and District 
staff
woul,d inspect and approve (or reject) 
the instal-Iation of
playground equipment, irrigation equipment, etc. fn short, there
is a sharing of responsibitity bet$reen the city and the District
staffs aLthough the City has ultimate responsibility for ensuring
compliance with building codes and design criteria.
For purposes of addressing the issues sought to be raised in
this request, please assume that the only invol-vement of the
District Board would be renderlng a decision on whether or not to
recommend construction of a park or receipt of in lieu fees under
the park dedication ordj-nance. Assume further that at the tirne
such a decision is to be made by the board, I,Ir. Bing has no idea
of whether or not he wifl be subnitting a bid to the developer
who is processing its tentative tract map through the City
approval process. In other words, nothing is definite enough foi
the developer at this juncture to have put out any part ot tfre
project for bids. 
Assume also that once the aeveloper has
completed the approval" process the construction of a park is
required and that White oak submits a successful Uia for
construction of the park. Please also assune that the anount of
the bid is i-n excess of g100,oOO.OO, and that Mr. Bing will 
be
compensated in excess of g25O.OO, for any work done on the
project. 
Assume also that the invofvenent ci District staff rvi11
be as described above.
Ouestions Raised.
Given the facts stated above, does the Act require
boardnember Bing to refrain 
from subrnitting bids for 
the
construction of such a 'turn keyrr park, and i-f the succlssful
bidder, from contracting with any aeveloper who mignt propose a
project to be buitt within the geograpliical uouna-arie-s -Jr tn"
District? . Further, .under tne ecti nrist- boardm"rn"i ai"g rlfrain

we try to answer atl advice requests promptly. . Therefore,
unless you; request poses particularly compl-ex legal questions, or
more iniorrnation is needed, you should expect a response within 21
working days if your request seeks fornal, written advice. If more
information is needed, the person assiqned to prepare a response
to your request will contact you shortly to advise you as to
information needed. If your request i.s for informal assi"stance,
we will answer it as quickly as we can' (see commission
Regulation L8329 (2 cal. code of .Regs. sec. 18329).)
You also should be aware that your letter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Dear Ms. Breeze:
I am writing concerning the above

to request your cooperation in revising
under which the opinion is issued.
captioned opinion issued
My purpose in writing is
the specific code section
Somehow, through rny unartful use of the English language, I
did not make it clear to you in my reguest for the opinion, that
I was specifically acting on behalf of and had been personally
authorized by Mr. Bing to request the opinion on behalf of Mr.
Bing.
In other words, it was and alvrays has been rny intention to
make a request for an opinion under regulation section 18329(b),
Formal Written Advice. 
I would appreciate it 
if you could
reissue your opinion or simply issue an amendment to the existing
opinion indicating that it is a response being issued pursuant to
Government Code Section 83114 (b) .
I apologize that this request is so many months after the
issuance of your opinion in JuIy, 1990. Though I noticed my
inadvertent and unartful use of the English 1anguage l-ast summer,
I only recently discovered my failure to request this revision to
the opinion. 
P1ease caII me if you have any questions or if I
may be of any assistance.
cooperation.
Thank you for your anticipated

sections.question: Given the facts stated above, does the Act require boardmember Bing to refrain from submitting bids for the construction of such a 'turn key' park, and if the successful bidder, from contracting with any developer who might propose a project to be built within the geographical boundaries of the District? Further, under the Act must boardmember Bing refrain from participating in board deliberation and/or decision making with respect to any such project and, if so, at what stage or stages of the project?

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a board member who is a principal stockholder in a landscaping contractor company must recuse himself from board decisions regarding park construction projects that his company might bid on, and whether his company is prohibited from submitting bids on such projects.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The document does not contain a formal FPPC conclusion/advice section; it is a request for advice dated April 25, 1990, accompanied by a May 1, 1990 acknowledgment letter and a May 9, 1991 follow-up letter requesting clarification of the code section under which the advice should be issued.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: Document contains the initial advice request with detailed factual background, an acknowledgment letter from FPPC, and a follow-up letter from the requesting attorney. The actual FPPC response/conclusion is not included in this document. Document appears to be part of a larger case file. The extraction of the 'question' field is verbatim from the 'Questions Raised' section of the letter.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Given the facts stated above, does the Act require boardmember Bing to refrain from submitting bids for the construction of such a 'turn key' park, and if the successful bidder, from contracting with any developer who might propose a project to be built within the geographical boundaries of the District? Further, under the Act must boardmember Bing refrain from participating in board deliberation and/or decision making with respect to any such project and, if so, at what stage or stages of the project?

CONCLUSION: The document does not contain a formal FPPC conclusion/advice section; it is a request for advice dated April 25, 1990, accompanied by a May 1, 1990 acknowledgment letter and a May 9, 1991 follow-up letter requesting clarification of the code section under which the advice should be issued.

embedding.summary: Michael E. Kyle requests FPPC advice on behalf of the Livermore Area Recreation and Park District regarding whether board member David Bing has a conflict of interest given his ownership stake in White Oak Landscape, which could bid on park construction projects that the board votes to recommend.
